stem: A particle traces the curve given parametrically by \( x(t) = [[a]]\cos t \), \( y(t) = [[a]]\sin t \) as \( t \) runs from \( [[t0]]^\circ \) to \( [[t1]]^\circ \). Find the length of the path traveled.
solution: Here \( x'(t)=-[[a]]\sin t \) and \( y'(t)=[[a]]\cos t \), so the speed \( \sqrt{x'(t)^{2}+y'(t)^{2}} = [[a]] \) is constant. Converting the angles to radians, the arc length is \( \int_{[[t0]]^\circ}^{[[t1]]^\circ} [[a]]\,dt = [[a]]\cdot\dfrac{([[t1]]-[[t0]])\pi}{180} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the angle sweep in DEGREES directly as if it were already in radians, skipping the \( \pi/180 \) conversion.
This is the full circumference of the circle of radius \( [[a]] \), ignoring that the particle only traces part of it.
This halves the correct arc length.
This doubles the correct arc length.
This is off by one from the correct arc length.

stem: Find the length of the polar curve \( r = [[a]] \) (a circle of radius \( [[a]] \)) for \( \theta \) from \( [[th0]]^\circ \) to \( [[th1]]^\circ \).
solution: The polar arc-length formula is \( L=\int\sqrt{r^{2}+(dr/d\theta)^{2}}\,d\theta \). Since \( r=[[a]] \) is constant, \( dr/d\theta=0 \), so the integrand is just \( [[a]] \). Converting the angles to radians, \( L = \int_{[[th0]]^\circ}^{[[th1]]^\circ} [[a]]\,d\theta = [[a]]\cdot\dfrac{([[th1]]-[[th0]])\pi}{180} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the angle sweep in DEGREES directly as if it were already in radians, skipping the \( \pi/180 \) conversion.
This is the full circumference of the circle, ignoring that only part of it is swept out.
This halves the correct arc length.
This doubles the correct arc length.
This is off by one from the correct arc length.

stem: A curve is given parametrically by \( x(t) = [[x0]] + [[p]]t \), \( y(t) = [[y0]] + [[q]]t \), for \( [[t0]] \le t \le [[t1]] \). Find the arc length of this curve.
solution: This is a line, so the speed \( \sqrt{x'(t)^{2}+y'(t)^{2}} = \sqrt{[[p]]^{2}+[[q]]^{2}} = [[hyp]] \) is constant. The arc length is \( \int_{[[t0]]}^{[[t1]]} [[hyp]]\,dt = [[hyp]]\cdot([[t1]]-[[t0]]) = [[answer]] \), matching the distance formula.
distractor_rationales: This uses only the \( x \)-component's rate \( [[p]] \) as the speed, ignoring the \( y \)-component.
This uses only the \( y \)-component's rate \( [[q]] \) as the speed, ignoring the \( x \)-component.
This adds the two component rates instead of combining them via the Pythagorean theorem.
This doubles the correct arc length.
This is off by one from the correct arc length.

stem: Find the length of the portion of the cardioid \( r = [[a]](1+\cos\theta) \) traced as \( \theta \) runs from \( 0 \) to \( [[theta1]]^\circ \).
solution: Using \( 1+\cos\theta = 2\cos^{2}(\theta/2) \), the polar speed \( \sqrt{r^{2}+(dr/d\theta)^{2}} \) simplifies to \( 2\cdot[[a]]\cos(\theta/2) \) (non-negative here since \( \theta/2 \le 90^\circ \)). The arc length is \( \int_{0}^{[[theta1]]^\circ} 2\cdot[[a]]\cos(\theta/2)\,d\theta = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the length of the ENTIRE cardioid (a full \( 360^\circ \) sweep), not just the given portion.
This is the length of exactly the HALF cardioid (\( 180^\circ \)), regardless of the actual angle given.
This treats the speed as the constant \( [[a]] \) (as for a circle), ignoring the \( \cos(\theta/2) \) factor entirely.
This doubles the correct arc length.
This is off by one from the correct arc length.
